title: Recognizing Tuberculosis: When to Suspect and Isolate Early
subtitle: A 54-year-old shelter resident admitted as pneumonia has a 4-week cough, weight loss, and night sweats. A cavitary chest x-ray raises tuberculosis. The nurse must recognize the risk, start airborne precautions promptly, and protect staff and contacts.

patient.name: R. Owens
patient.admitDate: Admitted from the ED as community-acquired pneumonia. Homeless shelter resident with a 4-week cough, weight loss, and night sweats. A cavitary chest x-ray now raises suspicion for pulmonary tuberculosis after time in a shared room.

vitals.temp: AIIR + N95; sputum AFB x3 + NAAT

labs.v: Cough >3 wk, weight loss, night sweats, shelter/HIV.
labs.r: Isolate on suspicion, not after results.
Surgical mask on patient when out of room.

labs.v: Rifampin: orange fluids + CYP inducer; ethambutol: vision.
3 negative AFB smears + improvement on therapy.

nursesNote: R. Owens, 54, was admitted from the ED as community-acquired pneumonia, but his risk profile — homeless shelter residence, a 4-week cough, weight loss, and night sweats — should itself raise suspicion for tuberculosis, and a cavitary upper-lobe infiltrate on chest x-ray confirms the concern. Because TB is airborne, the key teaching is to isolate on suspicion rather than waiting for results: the patient is moved to a negative-pressure airborne isolation room with the door closed, and staff wear a fit-tested N95 on every entry. Diagnosis is pursued with three sputum AFB smears (including early-morning specimens) plus a NAAT, and four-drug RIPE therapy is started with pyridoxine to prevent isoniazid neuropathy and baseline labs for hepatotoxicity surveillance. When the patient must leave the room, he wears a surgical mask (never an N95, which protects the wearer). TB is a reportable disease, so public health is notified to coordinate directly observed therapy and a contact investigation of the exposed roommate and staff; isolation continues until there are three negative AFB smears with clinical improvement on effective therapy.
providerOrders: Isolate on suspicion: negative-pressure airborne room, door closed, fit-tested N95 on entry
Collect three sputum AFB smears plus a NAAT (Xpert MTB/RIF)
Start RIPE therapy with pyridoxine; obtain baseline LFTs and vision testing
Place a surgical mask on the patient for any transport (never an N95)
Report to public health for DOT and contact tracing; continue isolation until criteria met

opts.text: Cough over 3 weeks with weight loss and night sweats in a shelter resident suggests TB
TB suspicion should trigger airborne precautions before sputum results return
An upper-lobe cavitary infiltrate is the classic chest x-ray finding
Sputum AFB smears and a NAAT confirm the diagnosis
Standard precautions are adequate for suspected pulmonary TB
HIV testing should be offered because TB and HIV often co-occur
Pulmonary TB requires a negative-pressure airborne isolation room with the door closed
Staff wear a fit-tested N95 respirator on every room entry
When the patient leaves the room, the patient wears a surgical mask (not an N95)
Exposed staff and contacts need TB testing and follow-up
An N95 should be placed on the patient for transport
TB is reportable and the health department coordinates DOT and contact tracing

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order the actions on suspecting TB

opts.text: Place the patient in a negative-pressure airborne isolation room
Don a fit-tested N95 and keep the door closed
Collect sputum for AFB smears and NAAT
Notify infection prevention and public health; start contact investigation
Begin RIPE therapy as ordered and monitor
Move the patient to a negative-pressure airborne isolation room
Wear a fit-tested N95 for every entry
Put a surgical mask on the patient for transport
Report the case to public health and start contact tracing
Keep the patient on standard precautions in a shared room
Place an N95 on the patient instead of a surgical mask
Leave the isolation door open for monitoring
Omit pyridoxine with isoniazid

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Isolate in a negative-pressure room with N95 and a closed door
Obtain sputum AFB smears and a NAAT
Start RIPE therapy with pyridoxine and baseline labs
Report to public health and arrange DOT
Investigate exposed staff and contacts
The patient is in a negative-pressure room and staff wear fit-tested N95s
A surgical mask is placed on the patient for the trip to radiology
TB is reported and the health department arranges DOT and contact tracing
Pyridoxine is given with isoniazid and LFTs are monitored
The patient remains in a shared room on standard precautions
An N95 is put on the patient during transport
Isolation is stopped after one dose of RIPE despite positive smears
The exposed roommate and staff are not tested

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Safety and Infection Control, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
